svg.text: The Structure of a Block Header

g.data-narration: The block header is an 80-byte data structure containing six fields that uniquely identify each block and enable blockchain security.
g.text: Six fields totaling exactly 80 bytes form the cryptographic identity of every block

g.data-narration: Version is a 4-byte field indicating which blockchain rules this block follows, enabling protocol upgrades and fork signaling.
Previous Block Hash is a 32-byte SHA-256 hash linking this block to its parent, creating the blockchain's chain structure.
g.text: 32 bytes - Hash of parent block header

g.data-narration: Merkle Root is a 32-byte hash that summarizes all transactions in the block using a tree structure for efficient verification.
g.text: 32 bytes - Root of transaction hash tree

g.data-narration: Timestamp is a 4-byte Unix epoch time marking when the block was created, used for difficulty adjustments every two weeks.
g.text: 4 bytes - Unix time of block creation

g.data-narration: Difficulty Target is a 4-byte threshold that the block hash must be below for the block to be considered valid by the network.
Nonce is a 4-byte counter that miners repeatedly change, hashing the header until finding a valid hash below the target.
The mining process repeatedly hashes the entire header using double SHA-256, varying only the nonce until success.
g.text: Mining Process: Finding a Valid Hash
Combine all six fields → Double SHA-256 hash → Check if below target → Vary nonce and repeat
This computational work secures the blockchain through Proof-of-Work consensus

g.data-narration: The chain linkage via previous block hash makes the blockchain tamper-evident, as altering any block requires re-mining all subsequent blocks.
g.text: Each block contains the previous block's hash, creating an immutable chain that reveals any tampering attempt

g.data-narration: The 80-byte header enables efficient transmission and SPV verification, while the Merkle root allows transaction inclusion proof without downloading the full blockchain.
g.text: Compact header size enables lightweight clients to verify transactions efficiently using Merkle proof

g.data-narration: Total size equals exactly 80 bytes: Version plus Previous Hash plus Merkle Root plus Timestamp plus Difficulty plus Nonce.
